title: Python Tricks
subtitle: A Buffet of Awesome Python Features
author: Dan Bader
excerpt: Python Tricks is a curated collection of short, self-contained essays on intermediate-to-advanced Python topics, written by Dan Bader and self-published in 2017. At around 296 pages, it covers the idiomatic patterns most working Python developers need — but rarely learn in sequence: the iterator protocol, context managers, decorators, generators, type hints, data classes, f-strings, exception best practices, packaging and distribution, CLI tools with Click and Typer, and (in later editions) the walrus operator and structural pattern matching. The book is organized as a buffet, not a curriculum: you can read individual tricks in any order.
keyIdeas: Python productivity comes from internalizing the 10-20 idioms that cover 80% of daily coding — most developers learn them slowly, through years of code review
The iterator protocol (__iter__, __next__) is the unifying mechanism behind for-loops, comprehensions, generators, and much of the standard library — understanding it unlocks the rest
Context managers (the with statement) are the correct pattern for resource lifecycle; @contextlib.contextmanager lets you write one as a single generator with a yield
Decorators are just higher-order functions that wrap a function; @functools.wraps preserves metadata, and parameterized decorators are three levels of nesting
Data classes remove boilerplate by generating __init__, __repr__, __eq__, and __hash__ from a class declaration — they are the missing feature Python needed in 2017
f-strings (PEP 498) are the fastest, most readable string formatting in Python 3.6+; prefer them over % formatting and str.format()
The walrus operator (:=, PEP 572) lets you assign inside expressions, reducing repeated lookups and allowing patterns like while (line := f.readline()):
Structural pattern matching (match/case, PEP 634 in 3.10) is Python's answer to switch/else-if, but more powerful — it unpacks sequences, maps, and class instances
Type hints are optional but increasingly standard; from typing import List, Optional, Protocol teaches the ecosystem; @dataclass + from __future__ import annotations is modern Python
CLI tools deserve first-class libraries: Click (composable, decorator-based) and Typer (Click built on type hints, auto-complete, auto-help) make argument parsing pleasant
Concurrency in Python is a toolkit, not a single tool: threading for I/O-bound blocking code, multiprocessing for CPU-bound work, asyncio for thousands of concurrent I/O operations
Python packaging has improved dramatically; a modern pyproject.toml with flit or poetry is all you need to distribute a package on PyPI
keyTakeaways: Use f-strings for all string formatting in Python 3.6+; they are faster than both % formatting and str.format() and more readable for any expression
Write context managers with @contextlib.contextmanager for the common case; the generator-with-yield pattern eliminates the boilerplate of __enter__/__exit__ classes
Decorators should use @functools.wraps to preserve __name__, __doc__, and __module__; without it, every decorated function looks identical in stack traces and help()
Use the walrus operator (:=) to avoid calling the same function twice: while (line := f.readline()): process(line), or if (n := len(items)) > 0: handle(n)
Prefer list/set/dict comprehensions over map/filter; for nested loops, use multiple for clauses; for conditions, add an if clause at the end
Type hints make code self-documenting and enable IDE support; start with from typing import List, Dict, Optional; migrate to X | None syntax and PEP 604 unions in Python 3.10+
Use @dataclass for classes that are mostly data containers; add field(default=...) and field(default_factory=list) for mutable defaults; use @dataclass(frozen=True) for immutable records
Match/case (Python 3.10+) with guard clauses and type unpacking is more readable than cascading if/elif when routing on type or structure; use it for command handlers and AST walking
Prefer Click or Typer over argparse for CLI tools; Typer auto-generates --help and --version from type hints and function signatures with zero boilerplate
For I/O-bound concurrency, start with threading.ThreadPoolExecutor or asyncio; for CPU-bound work, use multiprocessing.ProcessPoolExecutor; measure before optimizing
Exceptions should be specific (not bare except:); use Exception Group (Python 3.11+) or exceptiongroup package for fan-out/fan-in error handling; never silence exceptions silently
Package modern Python with pyproject.toml; flit is the simplest for single-module packages, poetry for projects with dependencies; publishing to PyPI takes two commands
whoShouldRead: Intermediate Python developers who know the syntax but want to internalize the idioms professional Pythonistas use without thinking
Developers coming from other languages (Java, C#, JavaScript) who need a quick survey of the Pythonic patterns that differ from mainstream OOP
Self-taught developers who have built things but never had a structured walkthrough of context managers, decorators, generators, and the data model
Backend engineers and data engineers writing Python scripts, ETL pipelines, APIs, or automation tools who want to write cleaner, faster, and more Pythonic code
Python learners who have completed a beginner's tutorial and are looking for the bridge to professional-quality code
whoShouldSkip: Absolute beginners — this book assumes you know Python syntax, how functions and classes work, and how to run a .py file
Senior Python engineers already fluent in Pythonic idioms — the tricks here will feel familiar; you want Ramalho's Fluent Python for depth
Developers looking for a comprehensive language reference — this is a curated essay collection, not a systematic tour of every language feature
Data scientists whose primary Python is NumPy, pandas, and Jupyter notebooks — the book covers general Python patterns, not the data stack
Readers who want deep coverage of advanced topics like metaclasses, C extensions, or the full typing module — those are beyond this book's scope
